record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Recorder (county-level registration began with territorial period; statewide registration 1925)

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Research Wiki — North Dakota Burned Counties; SHSND county records guides; North Dakota vital records gaps documented by ND Dept of Health
administrative_notes: North Dakota was part of Dakota Territory (1861-1889) and became a state November 2, 1889. Pre-statehood records are filed under Dakota Territory at SHSND and NARA Kansas City. Many counties were created from unorganized territory or parent counties in the 1870s-1900s — check parent county records for earlier periods. ND has no independent cities; all 53 counties are active. The state experienced massive immigration from Norway, Germany/Russia, and Canada (Métis) between 1870-1920.

core_repositories.source_name: State Historical Society of North Dakota (SHSND)
core_repositories.description: Primary state repository for historical records. Houses microfilmed county records (deeds, probate, vital records), manuscript collections, territorial-era government records, newspapers on microfilm, and photograph collections. Heritage Center research room in Bismarck.

Collaborative digital library providing access to photographs, documents, newspapers, and other primary sources from North Dakota and the northern Great Plains. Includes materials from SHSND, university libraries, and local historical societies.

State government records, territorial records, county records on microfilm, naturalization records, brand books, prison records, and state census schedules. Houses microfilmed county recorder and clerk of court records for most ND counties.
Genealogy resources including ND county histories, plat books, city directories, and interlibrary loan access to genealogical materials statewide.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including vital records (births, deaths, marriages), county records, naturalization records, cemetery records, and church records. Significant microfilm holdings from SHSND partnership.
Wiki guide to county-level records availability, with links to digitized microfilm for individual counties. Includes deed, probate, and naturalization records.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic ND newspapers. Includes papers in English, Norwegian, and German reflecting the state's immigrant press. Coverage varies but includes territorial-era papers from the 1870s forward.
Additional digitized newspaper holdings not in Chronicling America, including small-town weeklies that documented homestead proofs, naturalization notices, and community events across rural ND.

immigration_and_naturalization.description: Essential resource for ND genealogy — Germans from Russia settled extensively in central and western ND (Emmons, Logan, McIntosh, Grant, Hettinger, Adams, Stark counties and more). GRHS maintains village-of-origin databases, family group sheets, immigration records, and connections to Black Sea and Volga German colonies. Research library in Bismarck.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA Central Plains Region, Kansas City
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Federal naturalization records for Dakota Territory and North Dakota. Includes declarations of intention and petitions for naturalization filed in federal courts. Also holds homestead case files for ND land entries.

Microfilmed naturalization records from county district courts across ND. Many counties have declarations of intention and petitions from the 1880s-1950s. Critical for tracing immigrant origins — especially Norwegian, German-Russian, and Canadian settlers.

Manuscript collections, emigrant correspondence, bygdebok references, and published histories of Norwegian settlement in the Dakotas. Useful for tracing origins in specific Norwegian parishes. Complemented by Digitalarkivet (Norwegian Digital Archives) for parish records.

native_american_and_tribal_records.source_name: Standing Rock Sioux Tribe — Enrollment & Records
native_american_and_tribal_records.repository: Standing Rock Sioux Tribe, Fort Yates
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: Tribal enrollment records, allotment records, and agency records for Standing Rock Reservation (Sioux County and parts of Morton County in ND, plus SD portion). Bureau of Indian Affairs Standing Rock Agency records at NARA Kansas City. Dawes Act allotment files at NARA.

native_american_and_tribal_records.source_name: Three Affiliated Tribes (MHA Nation) — Fort Berthold
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: Records of the Mandan, Hidatsa, and Arikara Nation at Fort Berthold Reservation (Mountrail, McKenzie, McLean, Dunn, Mercer counties). BIA Fort Berthold Agency records at NARA Kansas City. Garrison Dam relocation records (1950s) at SHSND and tribal archives. MHA Nation Interpretive Center in New Town.

native_american_and_tribal_records.source_name: Turtle Mountain Band of Chippewa Indians
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: Tribal enrollment and allotment records for Turtle Mountain Reservation (Rolette County). Strong Métis/Chippewa heritage — many families have mixed French-Canadian and Ojibwe ancestry. McCumber Agreement (1904) rolls are key source. BIA Turtle Mountain Agency records at NARA.
Tribal records for Spirit Lake (Devils Lake Sioux) Reservation in Benson County. Fort Totten Indian Industrial School records (1891-1935) at NARA. BIA Spirit Lake Agency records at NARA Kansas City.

native_american_and_tribal_records.source_name: NARA — Bureau of Indian Affairs Records (ND)
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: Federal records of BIA agencies in ND: Standing Rock, Fort Berthold, Turtle Mountain, Fort Totten (Spirit Lake), and Devils Lake. Includes census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, school records, annuity rolls, and agency correspondence. Some digitized on Fold3 and Ancestry.

military_records.description: State military records including WWI and WWII service cards, National Guard records, and veterans' bonus applications. Territorial-era military records for frontier forts (Fort Abraham Lincoln, Fort Rice, Fort Buford, Fort Totten, Fort Union).
Burial records for veterans interred at the state veterans cemetery. Supplemented by Find A Grave and county cemetery inventories.

land_and_property.source_name: Bureau of Land Management — General Land Office Records
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents (homesteads, timber culture, preemption) for North Dakota. Searchable by name, county, land description. Most ND land was acquired through the Homestead Act (1862) — original patent documents available. Critical first step for any ND land research.

Homestead application case files (final certificates) contain valuable genealogical data: applicant's name, age, birthplace, family members, witnesses, and proof of residence. Many ND files include naturalization documentation for immigrant homesteaders. Available through NARA order or Fold3.

vital_records.source_name: North Dakota Department of Health — Vital Records
vital_records.description: State-level vital records: births (1893+), deaths (1893+), marriages (1925+). Early compliance was poor — many events before ~1907 were not registered. For pre-1925 marriages, check county recorder records. Historical records (75+ years for births, 50+ for deaths/marriages) may be available through SHSND.

church_and_cemetery.description: Microfilmed church records from congregations across ND. Lutheran (Norwegian Synod, German Evangelical), Catholic, and Reformed churches are especially well represented. Baptism, confirmation, marriage, and burial registers often predate civil vital records.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Find A Grave — North Dakota Cemeteries
church_and_cemetery.description: Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for ND. Rural township cemeteries are numerous and often the only surviving record for early settlers. Coverage varies by county — some fully photographed, others incomplete.

state_census_and_special_enumerations.source_name: Dakota Territory & North Dakota State Census Records
state_census_and_special_enumerations.description: Dakota Territory censuses (1860, 1870, 1880 federal; 1885 territorial) and North Dakota state censuses (1905, 1915, 1925) supplement federal decennial censuses. The 1885 territorial census and 1905/1915/1925 state censuses are especially valuable for tracking immigrant settlement patterns. Available on microfilm at SHSND and partially indexed on FamilySearch.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Publishes quarterly newsletter and maintains surname registry. Links to county-level historical and genealogical societies across ND.
Comprehensive research guide covering ND record types, repositories, county formation history, and research strategies. Includes links to digitized records and county-specific pages.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, obituary lookups, historical sketches, and county-specific research guides.

historical_counties_extinct.description: North Dakota has no extinct counties, but many modern counties were created from larger parent counties or unorganized territory during the territorial and early statehood periods (1870s-1910s). Researchers should trace county formation dates to identify parent jurisdictions for earlier records. Key parent counties include Pembina (1867, earliest), Buffalo (1864, briefly), Burleigh, Cass, Grand Forks, and several others that spawned daughter counties. SHSND maintains a county formation chart mapping all jurisdictional changes.

burned_counties_summary.notes: North Dakota has no counties officially classified as 'burned counties' in the genealogical sense (catastrophic courthouse destruction). Some counties experienced minor record losses — for example, Grand Forks County had record damage during the 1997 Red River flood — but no county lost the majority of its pre-disaster records. All 53 counties retain substantially complete recorder and court records from their organization dates forward.
